elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce a new curvature condition for high-codimension submanifolds of a Riemannian ambient space, called quasi-parallel mean curvature (QPMC).
elements.sent-2411-002: The class of submanifolds with QPMC includes all CMC hypersurfaces and submanifolds with parallel mean curvature.
elements.sent-2411-003: We use our notion of QPMC to prove that certain kinds of high-curvature regions which appear in geometric flows, called bubblesheets, can be placed in a suitable normal form.
elements.sent-2411-004: This follows from a more general result asserting that the manifold $\R^k \times \S^{n-k}$, equipped with any metric which is sufficiently close to the standard one, admits a canonical foliation by embedded $(n-k)$-spheres with QPMC.
elements.sent-2411-005: At the core of differential geometry is the notion that the important features of a space should remain invariant under changes of coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-006: However a space with some special structure may admit preferred coordinate systems, which reveal its features with particular clarity.
elements.sent-2411-007: A proven method for finding such distinguished parameterizations is to identify a foliation of the space by submanifolds which are canonically determined by its geometry.
elements.sent-2411-008: Foliations by hypersurfaces with constant mean curvature (CMC) have been used to parameterize the ends of asymptotically flat manifolds, leading to a definition of center of mass for isolated gravitating systems \cite{Huisken--Yau (see also the more recent \cite{Cederbaum--Sakovich and references therein).}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the Lorentzian setting, a foliation by maximal (vanishing mean curvature) hypersurfaces played a role in the first proof of the stability of the Minkowski spacetime \cite{Christodoulou--Klainerman .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In yet another direction, a canonical CMC foliation for `necks' (Riemannian manifolds which locally resemble $\R \times \S^{n-1}$ at some scale), constructed by Hamilton, has been employed to continue geometric flows through neck singularities via surgery \cite{Hamilton_PIC, Huisk-Sin09 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this paper we construct a new canonical foliation for another kind of geometry which forms near singularities of both the Ricci flow and the mean curvature flow.
elements.sent-2411-012: These geometries, called bubblesheets, locally resemble $\R^k \times \S^{n-k}$ at some scale, where $k\geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-013: Bubblesheets are expected to be the most prevalent high-curvature regions in the mean curvature flow of hypersurfaces of dimension $n \geq 3$, and in the Ricci flow on manifolds of dimension $n \geq 4$.
elements.sent-2411-014: The essential question we address is the following: \begin{question Consider the manifold $\mathbb{R ^k \times \S^{n-k}$ equipped with the standard product metric $g_0 := g_{\R^k \oplus g_{\S^{n-k }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: This space is foliated by round, totally geodesic $(n-k)$-spheres, namely the slices $\{z\}\times\S^{n-k}$.
elements.sent-2411-016: If $g_0$ is deformed to a nearby metric, can these totally geodesic spheres be deformed into a canonical foliation of the resulting space? \end{question The foliation we seek is by spheres of codimension $k \geq 2$ which, by analogy with Hamilton's CMC foliation for $k =1$, ought to solve some equation involving their mean curvature.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The main challenge is to determine the right equation, or in this case system of equations, since our spheres have codimension greater than one.
elements.sent-2411-018: One might seek a foliation by leaves whose mean curvature has constant length, or is parallel in the normal bundle.
elements.sent-2411-019: Neither of these conditions works; in a certain sense they are underdetermined and overdetermined respectively.
elements.sent-2411-020: The correct approach, we argue, is to foliate by spheres which have quasi-parallel mean curvature (QPMC).
elements.sent-2411-021: This new curvature condition is more general than having parallel mean curvature, but almost as restrictive (as it must be if our foliation is to be canonical).
elements.sent-2411-022: To see what we mean by QPMC, fix a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ of dimension $n$ and consider a submanifold $\Sigma \subset M$ of codimension $k$.
elements.sent-2411-023: The Levi-Civita connection of $(M,g)$ induces a connection $\nabla^\perp$ and hence a Laplacian $\Delta^\perp$ acting on sections of the normal bundle $N\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-024: The operator $-\Delta^\perp$ admits a complete set of eigensections with nonnegative eigenvalues $\lambda_m = \lambda_m(\Sigma, g)$, labelled in nondecreasing order, repeating according to multiplicity.
elements.sent-2411-025: We write $P_\lambda$ for the $\RL^2$-orthogonal projection onto the eigenspace associated with $\lambda \in \spec(-\Delta^\perp)$ and define \begin{equation}Q := \sum_{\substack{ \lambda \in \spec(-\Delta^\perp) \lambda < \lambda_{k+1} }} P_{\lambda}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-026: A section of $N\Sigma$ is called quasi-parallel if it lies in $\im(Q)$, and so the submanifold $\Sigma$ is said to have QPMC if its mean curvature vector $H$ satisfies \begin{equation}\label{QPMC} (1 - Q)(H) = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-027: We note that $1-Q$ is a nonlocal pseudodifferential operator acting on sections of $N\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Expressed as $H = Q(H)$, \eqref{QPMC is a weakly elliptic quasilinear system for the position of the submanifold, with nonlocal right-hand side.}
elements.sent-2411-029: From a variational point of view, $\Sigma$ has QPMC if and only if $\Sigma$ is a critical point for the volume functional with respect to variations whose initial velocity lies in $\ker(Q)$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Let us further clarify the meaning of \eqref{QPMC with a few remarks:.}
elements.sent-2411-031: If $\Sigma$ is of codimension 1 and 2-sided, then $Q$ is the projection onto the 1-dimensional space of harmonic sections of $N\Sigma$ (normal vectors of constant length).
elements.sent-2411-032: Therefore, in this setting $\Sigma$ has CMC if and only if it has QPMC.
elements.sent-2411-033: Since $\Sigma$ is compact, $\ker(\Delta^\perp)$ is precisely the space of parallel sections of $N\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-034: In particular, if $H$ is parallel in $N\Sigma$, meaning $\nabla^\perp H =0$, then $\Sigma$ has QPMC.
elements.sent-2411-035: When $k \geq 2$, for a general submanifold $\Sigma$, the normal bundle $N\Sigma$ may admit no global nonzero parallel sections.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, if $\lambda_k < \lambda_{k+1}$ (which is true generically), then $\im(Q)$ is $k$-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-037: In a sense which can be made precise using Rayleigh quotients, $\im(Q)$ is the $k$-dimensional subspace of normal vector fields which are `as close as possible' to being parallel.
